ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for bara chirashi don or spicy salmon don combo. FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM (fish and seafood-based dishes) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for chirashi don or spicy salmon don combo in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific Japanese seafood rice bowl combination dish.

preparation_method: sushi rice prepared with rice vinegar
main_ingredients: assorted sashimi-grade seafood (tuna, yellowtail, scallop, prawn)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, representing a full donburi bowl containing roughly 200 g of sushi rice and 250 g of mixed seafood toppings split equally between bara chirashi and spicy salmon components.

nutrient_source.title: Chirashi Sushi / Scattered Sushi Rice Bowl — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Salmon, raw (FDC ID 175167), White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756), and Mayonnaise (FDC ID 172741), weighted by estimated ingredient proportions for a half-chirashi half-spicy-salmon don bowl.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for bara chirashi don or spicy salmon don exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 44% cooked sushi rice, 40% mixed raw seafood (salmon dominant), 8% spicy mayonnaise sauce, and 8% garnishes (avocado, cucumber, tobiko, nori, sesame). Macros cross-checked against published Japanese food composition data (Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan, 2020, MEXT) for chirashi sushi category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021;44(5):e1–e13. Sushi rice / vinegared rice GI reference: GI approximately 52 (white rice, boiled, vinegared, sushi-style).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bara chirashi don or spicy salmon don combo. GI of 52 is based on the analogous food 'sushi rice (vinegared white rice)' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat content from seafood toppings further attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain rice. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 41.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.5 × 4.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 280 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced soy sauce, as the sodium load from soy sauce and seasoned sushi rice is clinically significant; pairing with low-sodium sides and limiting additional soy sauce dipping is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 41 g GL units due to the substantial sushi rice component, despite a moderate GI of 52 for the rice base; the protein and omega-3 fatty acids from salmon partially attenuate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the large carbohydrate load from sushi rice in a full bowl serving; portion reduction to half a bowl or substituting with cauliflower rice where available, combined with the beneficial effect of salmon protein and fat on glycemic response, can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from fresh salmon and other seafood; saturated fat is low at approximately 0.9 g per 100 g; however, sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and seasoned rice.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high omega-3 content from salmon provides cardioprotective benefits including reduction of triglycerides and anti-inflammatory effects; individuals with cardiovascular disease should enjoy this dish while requesting minimal added soy sauce to manage sodium intake within heart-healthy limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Bara Chirashi Don Half Spicy Salmon Don is a Japanese seafood rice bowl (donburi) dish that combines two popular styles in a single serving. The 'bara chirashi' half features a colourful assortment of sashimi-grade seafood pieces — typically including salmon, tuna, yellowtail, scallop, and prawn — scattered over seasoned sushi rice (vinegared short-grain rice), garnished with tobiko, cucumber, avocado, nori, and sesame seeds. The 'spicy salmon' half features diced or sliced fresh salmon dressed in a spicy mayonnaise-based sauce (typically sriracha or chilli mayo), also served over sushi rice. The dish is served cold or at room temperature, as is traditional for chirashi and spicy salmon don preparations. The texture profile combines the tender, buttery quality of raw salmon with the slight chew of sushi rice, the crunch of cucumber and sesame, and the creamy heat of the spicy sauce. Nutritionally, this dish is a good source of high-quality protein (approximately 9.2 g per 100 g), omega-3 fatty acids from salmon, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and selenium. The carbohydrate content is moderate (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g) with a low-to-moderate glycemic index of 52 for the sushi rice base. Sodium is a clinical consideration at approximately 280 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoned rice. This dish is suitable for most healthy adults and is particularly valued for its omega-3 content, but individuals with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and soy sauce use. It is not suitable for those with fish, shellfish, soy, egg, or sesame allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for this specific half-and-half combo dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central individual ingredient entries (salmon raw, cooked white rice, mayonnaise) and cross-checked against the Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan (MEXT 2020) chirashi sushi category. GI is analogously estimated from sushi rice data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and restaurant-specific variation in ingredient ratios.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, rice-to-topping ratio, and sauce quantity. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as salmon and rice-based dishes contain negligible industrially-produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. Sodium estimate of 280 mg per 100 g accounts for soy sauce seasoning in sushi rice and light soy sauce accompaniment; actual sodium may be higher if additional soy sauce is used at the table. 4. GI of 52 is based on analogous sushi rice data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The cuisine_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' in the source input appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it does not describe this food. 6. Halal status is not confirmed as the spicy mayonnaise sauce may contain non-halal ingredients depending on the restaurant. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for salmon (raw) and cooked white rice; these are reasonable estimates but carry medium uncertainty.
